
\color{blue}

**Solution**. D.\

It is possible that the search of parameter space has missed the region with parameter values that match the data well. However, after a reasonbly well-conducted global search, it is usually right to take your results at face value as a good approximation to the global maximum. If you are worried about ability to maximize, a simulation study (re-estimating with known parameters) is a good check. So, (A) is not the winner.

(B) can also be true, but is not a constructive next step.

(C) is also, broadly speaking, good advice. However, we don't want to spend too much time and effort on redesigning our model until we are more sure that there is indeed a substantial problem.

(D) is good advice. If the model has competitive log-likelihood versus alternative models, it is worth considering carefully whether the problem is with our judgement of the visual comparison rather than with the model itself.

Residual plots can help with diagnostics, especially to identify outliers. However, they are only indirectly related to the question at hand in this question.

\color{black}
